name: Basilica of the Transfiguration
description: The main church of the monastery, built by order of Emperor Justinian I between 548 and 565 AD, making it one of the oldest continuously functioning churches in the world. Contains stunning 6th-century mosaic of the Transfiguration of Christ in the apse — one of the finest surviving Byzantine mosaics. The carved wooden doors and marble columns are original from the 6th century. Houses an extraordinary collection of icons spanning 1,500 years.

sections.title: Transfiguration Mosaic
sections.content: The stunning 6th-century apse mosaic depicting Christ's Transfiguration on Mount Tabor — one of the oldest and finest Byzantine mosaics surviving anywhere in the world, with vivid gold, blue, and green tesserae.

sections.title: Icon Collection
sections.content: The monastery holds the world's finest collection of early Christian icons, including rare encaustic (wax-painted) icons from the 6th century that survived the Byzantine Iconoclasm because the monastery's remote location placed it beyond the emperor's reach.

name: Chapel of the Burning Bush
description: Located behind the apse of the main church, this small chapel is built over the spot where tradition holds the Burning Bush stood — from which God spoke to Moses. A living bush (Rubus sanctus) still grows beside the chapel and is venerated by visitors. The chapel floor has a silver plaque marking the sacred spot, and visitors must remove their shoes, recalling God's command to Moses. The site is sacred to Christians, Jews, and Muslims.

name: Church of Saints Sergius and Bacchus (Abu Serga)
description: One of the oldest churches in Egypt, built over the crypt where the Holy Family is believed to have sheltered during their flight to Egypt. The underground crypt (accessible when not flooded) is a pilgrimage site. The basilica plan with 12 columns (one of dark granite, representing Judas) is typical of early Coptic architecture.

name: The Hanging Church (Al-Mu'allaqa)
description: Cairo's most beautiful church, 'suspended' above the gatehouse of the Roman Babylon Fortress on 15 stone pillars. Dedicated to the Virgin Mary, its nave features 110 ivory-inlaid icons, a 13th-century marble pulpit resting on 13 columns (representing Christ and the 12 apostles), and a 7th-century wooden roof shaped like Noah's Ark.

name: Abu Mena
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site — the ruins of the early Christian holy city built over the grave of Saint Menas, a 3rd-century Egyptian martyr. Once one of the greatest pilgrimage centers in the early Christian world, the site includes the remains of a great basilica, a baptistery, baths, and a monastery. Pilgrims came from across the Mediterranean seeking miraculous healing from the sacred waters. The characteristic two-handled flask (ampulla) with Saint Menas praying between two camels became one of the most widely distributed devotional objects in the ancient world.

name: Al-Azhar Mosque & University
description: The oldest university in the world (founded 970 CE) and one of Cairo's most important mosques. Built by the Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli shortly after founding Cairo. The name means 'The Splendid' — a reference to Fatimah al-Zahra. Its courtyard blends Fatimid, Mamluk, and Ottoman architectural layers accumulated over a millennium.

name: Al-Muizz li-Din Allah Street
description: The spine of historic Cairo — a 1-km open-air museum stretching from Bab al-Futuh to Bab Zuweila. Lined with the highest concentration of medieval Islamic architecture in the world: mosques, madrasas, hammams, sabils (public fountains), and caravanserais. Best explored at night when the monuments are illuminated and the street is pedestrianized.

name: Historic Bazaar & Souqs
description: One of the oldest and largest bazaars in the Middle East, founded by Mamluk Emir Djaharks el-Khalili in 1382. A labyrinth of narrow alleys packed with shops selling gold and silver jewelry, brass and copperware, spices, perfumes, papyrus, alabaster, leather goods, and traditional handicrafts. The architecture preserves Mamluk-era caravanserais (merchants' inns) and wakalahs (trade buildings). Bargaining is expected and part of the experience.

sections.title: Gold & Silver Souq
sections.content: A dedicated alley of goldsmiths and jewelers selling exquisite handcrafted pieces — from traditional Egyptian cartouche pendants to modern designs. Gold is sold by weight plus craftsmanship.

sections.title: Spice Souq
sections.content: Aromatic stalls piled high with saffron, cumin, hibiscus, dried roses, and Egyptian-blend spice mixes. Many shops also sell traditional herbal remedies and Egyptian perfume oils.

name: Mosque of Muhammad Ali
description: The landmark Ottoman-style mosque dominating Cairo's skyline, built by Muhammad Ali Pasha between 1830 and 1848. Its twin minarets rise 82 meters and its massive central dome spans 21 meters. The interior is lavishly decorated with alabaster panels (earning it the name 'Alabaster Mosque'), ornate chandeliers, and gilded calligraphy. A clock tower gifted by French King Louis-Philippe stands in the courtyard — given in exchange for the Luxor Obelisk now in Paris.

sections.title: The Alabaster Interior
sections.content: The walls up to 11 meters are clad in fine alabaster, while the upper sections and dome are painted with elaborate Ottoman decorative motifs. Massive chandeliers illuminate the prayer hall.

sections.title: The French Clock
sections.content: An ornamental clock in the courtyard, a gift from Louis-Philippe of France in 1845, exchanged for the 3,300-year-old Luxor Obelisk now standing in Place de la Concorde, Paris. The clock has never worked — some say it arrived broken.

name: Mosque-Madrassa of Sultan Hassan
description: A masterpiece of Mamluk architecture and one of the largest mosques in the world. The 36-meter-high entrance portal is the tallest in Cairo. The cruciform plan has four iwans around a central courtyard with an ablution fountain. The qibla iwan's 32-meter vault was the largest in Egypt. Some limestone blocks were allegedly taken from the Great Pyramid's casing.

name: Hot Air Balloon over Luxor
description: One of the world's most breathtaking aerial experiences — floating over the Valley of the Kings, Temple of Hatshepsut, Medinet Habu, the Ramesseum, and the Colossi of Memnon at sunrise. Balloons launch from the west bank of the Nile at dawn, rising to 300-500 meters for panoramic views of the patchwork of green sugarcane fields, desert mountains, and 3,500-year-old temples bathed in golden morning light. The contrast between the lush Nile Valley and the barren Theban Hills is unforgettable. Approximately 30 balloons operate daily in season, carrying thousands of visitors each year.

name: Main Reading Hall
description: The centerpiece of the Bibliotheca — a spectacular 11-story cascading reading room tilted toward the Mediterranean, covering 70,000 square meters with space for 8 million books and 2,000 readers. The circular design references the ancient Egyptian sun disk descending into the sea. The exterior granite wall is carved with characters from 120 different human scripts — a monument to human knowledge. Designed by Norwegian architecture firm Snohetta, it is one of the most striking modern buildings in the Middle East.

sections.title: The Cascading Design
sections.content: The reading room cascades across 11 levels beneath a massive 160-meter-diameter glass-paneled roof, tilted toward the Mediterranean — symbolizing the ancient sun disk Ra descending into the sea, representing knowledge enlightening the world.

sections.title: The Script Wall
sections.content: The exterior Aswan granite wall is engraved with characters from 120 different writing systems from around the world — from Egyptian hieroglyphs to modern scripts — celebrating the universality of human knowledge.

name: Nile Cruise (Luxor to Aswan)
description: The quintessential Egyptian experience — a multi-day cruise along the Nile between Luxor and Aswan (or vice versa), tracing the route of pharaohs and Victorian-era explorers. Most cruises span 3-7 nights, stopping at Edfu Temple, Kom Ombo Temple, and the Esna Lock along the way. Passengers wake to ever-changing riverscapes of palm-fringed banks, feluccas gliding past, and ancient temples emerging from the horizon. Options range from luxurious 5-star floating hotels to traditional dahabiyas (wooden sailing boats). The journey offers a unique perspective on rural Egyptian life along the fertile Nile Valley.

name: Grand Egyptian Museum (GEM)
description: The world's largest archaeological museum dedicated to a single civilization, located 2 km from the Giza Pyramids. At a cost of $1.2 billion, the GEM houses over 100,000 artifacts across 12 permanent galleries arranged chronologically from prehistory to the Greco-Roman period. Its crown jewel is the complete 5,398-piece Tutankhamun collection displayed together for the first time. The museum spans 500,000 square meters with a dramatic glass-roofed atrium housing an 11-meter, 83-ton Ramesses II colossus, a monumental grand staircase with 60+ artifacts, the reconstructed Khufu Solar Boats, a children's museum with AR experiences, and 19 conservation laboratories. Officially inaugurated on November 1, 2025 — the anniversary of Tutankhamun's tomb discovery — it received 18,000 visitors on its first public day.

name: Grand Hall (Atrium)
description: The spectacular main entrance hall spanning 10,000 square meters with a glass roof and transparent facade offering views of the Giza Pyramids from inside. Dominated by the 11-meter, 83-ton red granite colossus of Ramesses II — moved from Memphis via Ramses Square in 2006 — alongside 20-30 monumental artifacts from different eras serving as an introductory exhibition to pharaonic civilization.

sections.title: Ramesses II Colossus
sections.content: This 83-ton, 11-meter red granite statue was discovered in 1820 at Mit Rahina (ancient Memphis). It stood in Ramses Square, Cairo for decades before careful relocation to the GEM in 2006, where it was placed in the Grand Hall in 2018.

sections.title: Glass Facade & Pyramid Views
sections.content: The building's design by Heneghan Peng Architects creates a chamfered triangle shape, with north and south walls aligned directly to the Great Pyramid of Khufu and the Pyramid of Menkaure. The translucent alabaster stone facade is decorated with cartouches bearing hieroglyphic names of kings and queens.

sections.title: Monumental Artifacts
sections.content: The atrium displays 20-30 large-scale artifacts from different eras, creating a chronological introduction to Egyptian civilization for visitors before they enter the main galleries.

name: Grand Staircase
description: A monumental transitional gallery spanning 6,000 square meters and six stories (approximately 50 meters high), connecting the main atrium to the 12 exhibition halls via the upper terrace. Displays over 60 artifacts arranged in four thematic sections — Royal Image, Divine Houses, Gods & Kings, and Funerary — creating a chronological journey through pharaonic civilization with another stunning view of the Giza Pyramids.

sections.title: Royal Image
sections.content: The first thematic section focuses on how and why pharaohs portrayed themselves, featuring royal statues spanning all periods of Egyptian history.

sections.title: Divine Houses
sections.content: Explores temples and the relationship between gods and pharaohs, with religious artwork and architectural fragments from major temple complexes.

sections.title: Gods & Kings
sections.content: Examines ancient Egyptian deities and their association with rulers, including divine statuary and votive objects.

sections.title: Funerary Section
sections.content: Displays stone coffins and sarcophagi from various eras, including a pyramid-shaped capstone (pyramidion) from the 13th Dynasty, illustrating evolving burial practices.

name: Narmer Palette & Early Dynastic
description: Home to the Narmer Palette — one of the oldest historical documents in the world, depicting the unification of Upper & Lower Egypt around 3100 BC. Also includes the Scorpion Macehead, ivory knife handles from Abydos, and other Pre-Dynastic artifacts that mark the birth of pharaonic civilization.

sections.title: The Narmer Palette
sections.content: A 64 cm ceremonial slate palette showing King Narmer wearing both the White Crown of Upper Egypt and Red Crown of Lower Egypt — symbolizing the first unification around 3100 BC.

sections.title: Scorpion Macehead
sections.content: A limestone macehead depicting King Scorpion performing an irrigation ceremony, pre-dating the unification.

name: National Museum of Egyptian Civilization
description: A museum in Fustat spanning all of Egyptian civilization from prehistory to modern times, home to the Royal Mummies Hall with 22 pharaonic mummies.

name: Royal Mummies Hall
description: The solemn underground gallery housing 22 royal mummies transferred from the Egyptian Museum in a spectacular televised 'Pharaohs Golden Parade' on April 3, 2021, with 18 kings and 4 queens carried in nitrogen-filled capsules through Cairo. Includes Ramesses II, Hatshepsut, Seti I, Thutmose III, Ahmose I, and Merenptah. Each mummy is displayed in an individual climate-controlled case with biographical information and CT scan results.

sections.title: Pharaohs Golden Parade
sections.content: On April 3, 2021, 22 royal mummies were transported from the Egyptian Museum to NMEC in a spectacular parade through Cairo, each in a custom nitrogen-filled capsule on a decorated chariot-style vehicle, accompanied by a 21-gun salute.

sections.title: Ramesses II
sections.content: The mummy of Ramesses the Great (r. 1279–1213 BC), one of Egypt's most powerful pharaohs, showing he lived to approximately 90 years. His well-preserved remains reveal a tall man with red hair.

sections.title: Queen Hatshepsut
sections.content: Egypt's most successful female pharaoh, identified through DNA testing and a matching tooth found in a canopic jar. She ruled for 21 years during the 18th Dynasty.

name: Tutankhamun Galleries
description: The crown jewel of the GEM — two dedicated halls covering 7,000 square meters housing the complete 5,398-piece Tutankhamun collection, displayed together for the first time ever. Previously only a fraction was shown at the Egyptian Museum in Tahrir. The halls are designed to evoke the original tomb in the Valley of the Kings, with environmental controls protecting sensitive gold, wood, and fabric pieces.

sections.title: The Golden Mask
sections.content: The iconic 11 kg solid gold death mask, inlaid with lapis lazuli, turquoise, and carnelian. Discovered by Howard Carter in 1925 within the innermost coffin. The central piece of the entire museum.

sections.title: The Nested Coffins
sections.content: Three anthropoid coffins — the innermost made of 110 kg of solid gold, the two outer ones of gold-plated wood. The craftsmanship represents the peak of New Kingdom goldsmithing. Note: Tutankhamun's mummy itself remains in the Valley of the Kings.

sections.title: The Golden Throne
sections.content: An ornate wooden throne overlaid with gold and silver, depicting Tutankhamun with his wife Ankhesenamun in the intimate Amarna art style inherited from Akhenaten's revolutionary period.

sections.title: Canopic Shrine & Jars
sections.content: The elaborate gilded canopic shrine containing four miniature gold coffins that held the king's preserved organs, each guarded by a protective goddess.

sections.title: Royal Chariots & Weapons
sections.content: Six chariots including ceremonial and military designs, plus daggers (one of meteoritic iron), bows, and other weapons — the first complete ancient Egyptian chariot collection ever discovered.

sections.title: Daily Life Objects
sections.content: Furniture including beds and chairs, dining utensils, board games, and over 400 ushabti figurines meant to serve the king in the afterlife. These objects reveal intimate details of royal daily life 3,300 years ago.

name: Tutankhamun Room (Historical Display)
description: The museum's most famous room historically displayed the golden mask and treasures of Tutankhamun for nearly a century. While the main collection has transferred to the GEM, select pieces and the room's historical significance remain. Archival displays tell the story of Howard Carter's 1922 discovery.

name: Blue Hole
description: One of the world's most famous dive sites — a 130-meter-deep submarine sinkhole in the Red Sea coast near Dahab. Known for its crystalline blue water, dramatic coral walls, and 'The Arch' passage at 56 meters connecting to the open sea. Popular with freedivers and scuba divers of all levels (shallow reef sections are safe for snorkelers), though its depth has earned it a reputation as one of the most dangerous dive sites. The surrounding coral reef is spectacularly healthy and teeming with marine life.

name: Cleopatra's Spring (Ain Juba)
description: A large natural spring pool fed by underground aquifers, where locals and visitors swim year-round in the bubbling 28°C water. Legend (unsubstantiated) says Cleopatra bathed here. Surrounded by palm trees, it's the social heart of Siwa — best enjoyed at sunset when the palms create dramatic silhouettes.

name: Mushroom Rock Formations
description: Surreal white chalk formations sculpted by wind erosion into shapes resembling giant mushrooms, sphinxes, and animals. Rising from the flat desert floor, these formations glow orange at sunset and gleam under moonlight. The most photographed include the 'Chicken and Tree' and the 'White Sphinx'. Camping among them under a star-blanket sky is an unforgettable desert experience.

description: Two massive rock-cut temples built by Ramesses II, famously relocated in the 1960s to save them from Lake Nasser's rising waters. The sun illuminates the inner sanctuary twice yearly.

name: Aga Khan Mausoleum
description: A stunning pink limestone mausoleum on the west bank of the Nile in Aswan, built for Aga Khan III (Sultan Muhammad Shah), the 48th Imam of the Ismaili Muslims who loved Aswan and spent winters there. The elegant Fatimid-style domed tomb is modeled on the tombs of the Fatimid Caliphs in Cairo. A single red rose was placed on the white marble sarcophagus every day by his widow, the Begum, until her death in 2000. The hilltop location offers spectacular panoramic views of the Nile, Elephantine Island, and the surrounding desert.

name: Avenue of Sphinxes (Sphinx Alley)
description: A 2.7-km ceremonial road connecting Luxor Temple to Karnak Temple, lined with over 1,350 sphinx statues (mostly ram-headed near Karnak, human-headed near Luxor). Restored and reopened in November 2021 in a spectacular ceremony. Walking this path at sunset is one of Luxor's most atmospheric experiences.

name: Bent Pyramid of Sneferu
description: The world's best-preserved pyramid, still retaining most of its original Tura limestone casing. Its distinctive shape — the angle changes from 54° to 43° partway up — reveals a mid-construction crisis when engineers feared collapse. This 'mistake' created one of Egypt's most recognizable silhouettes and provides crucial evidence of pyramid-building evolution.

name: Colossus of Ramesses II
description: A 10-meter limestone statue of Ramesses II lying in a purpose-built indoor museum. Originally 13 meters tall (the lower legs are missing), it was found in 1820 by Giovanni Caviglia. Visitors view it from a raised gallery, appreciating the extraordinary detail — from the pharaoh's nemes headdress to individual fingers. A twin statue once stood here; it's now in front of the Grand Egyptian Museum.

name: Court of Ramesses II
description: The first court upon entering the temple, surrounded by 74 papyrus-bud columns in a double row. Originally fronted by six colossal statues of Ramesses II (only two survive) and two obelisks — one now stands in the Place de la Concorde in Paris (gifted to France in 1829). The Abu El Haggag Mosque sits atop the northeast corner, built when the court was buried under centuries of sediment.

name: Great Hypostyle Hall
description: The most awe-inspiring interior space of the ancient world — 134 massive stone columns covering 5,000 sq meters. The 12 central columns rise 24 meters tall with open papyrus capitals, while 122 flanking columns stand 15 meters with closed bud capitals. Built by Seti I and Ramesses II. The forest of carved columns, once vibrantly painted, still bears traces of original color.

name: Great Hypostyle Hall
description: The stunning entrance hall of the Temple of Hathor featuring 24 massive columns topped with four-faced Hathor capitals — the goddess's face bearing cow ears on each side. The ceiling retains remarkable original astronomical decoration in vivid blues, showing the sky goddess Nut swallowing and rebirthing the sun, zodiac figures, and vultures. Added by the Roman Emperor Tiberius, the hall's rich color makes it one of the best-preserved temple interiors in Egypt.

sections.title: Hathor-Headed Columns
sections.content: 24 towering columns each topped with the face of Hathor on all four sides — a forest of goddess faces unique to Dendera. The capitals were originally painted in vivid colors.

sections.title: Astronomical Ceiling
sections.content: The ceiling preserves extraordinary astronomical decoration — the sky goddess Nut stretches across the full length, with zodiac figures, decans (star groups), and detailed depictions of the sun's daily cycle.

name: Great Pylon & Courtyard
description: The 36-meter-high pylon — the best-preserved temple gateway in Egypt — featuring enormous reliefs of Ptolemy XII smiting enemies before Horus. Two black granite falcon statues of Horus wearing the double crown guard the entrance. Beyond lies a spacious columned courtyard surrounded by 32 columns with varied floral capitals.

sections.title: The Horus Falcons
sections.content: Two magnificent black granite statues of Horus as a falcon wearing the double crown of Upper and Lower Egypt — among the most iconic images of ancient Egypt.

sections.title: Festival Courtyard
sections.content: The open courtyard was the setting for the annual Beautiful Festival of Reunion, when the statue of Hathor traveled from Dendera to 'visit' Horus at Edfu.

name: Great Pyramid of Khufu (Cheops)
description: The oldest and only surviving Wonder of the Ancient World. Originally 146.6 meters tall (now 138.5m), built from 2.3 million limestone blocks averaging 2.5 tons each. The internal King's Chamber contains a red granite sarcophagus. The precision of construction — sides aligned to true north within 3/60th of a degree — remains astonishing.

sections.title: King's Chamber
sections.content: The main burial chamber, constructed entirely of red granite from Aswan. Contains a lidless granite sarcophagus. Five 'relieving chambers' above distribute the immense weight.

sections.title: Queen's Chamber
sections.content: A misnomer — likely a serdab (statue chamber). Features a corbelled niche in the east wall and two mysterious 'air shafts' that don't reach the exterior.

sections.title: Grand Gallery
sections.content: A stunning 47-meter ascending corbelled passage, 8.6 meters high. Its precise construction and purpose — perhaps a counterweight mechanism — continues to be debated.

name: Great Pyramids of Giza
description: The last surviving wonder of the ancient world — three monumental pyramids built for pharaohs Khufu, Khafre, and Menkaure on the Giza Plateau.

name: Great Sphinx of Giza
description: The largest monolithic statue in the world — a lion's body with a human head, carved directly from the limestone bedrock of the Giza plateau. Measures 73 meters long, 20 meters high, and 19 meters wide. Believed to represent Pharaoh Khafre, it has guarded the Giza necropolis for 4,500 years. The 'Dream Stele' between its paws tells of Thutmose IV's vision.

sections.title: The Missing Nose
sections.content: Contrary to popular myth, Napoleon did not shoot off the Sphinx's nose. Historical evidence points to Muhammad Sa'im al-Dahr, a Sufi Muslim, who vandalized it in 1378 CE.

sections.title: Dream Stele
sections.content: A granite stele erected between the Sphinx's paws by Thutmose IV (1401 BC), recording his dream that the Sphinx promised him the throne if he cleared the sand.

name: Great Temple of Ramesses II
description: The larger of the two Abu Simbel temples, fronted by four colossal 20-meter statues of Ramesses II. Cut entirely into the rock cliff above the Nile. The interior extends 56 meters into the mountain with a Great Hall of eight Osiride pillars, battle reliefs of Kadesh, and the famous 'Sun Festival' sanctuary — twice a year (Feb 22, Oct 22) sunlight penetrates 60 meters to illuminate three of the four statues in the inner sanctum.

images.caption: Great Temple of Ramesses II (at Abu Simbel Temples)

Great Temple of Ramesses II — Abou Simbel (DOI 957)

sections.title: The Sun Festival
sections.content: On February 22 and October 22 each year, sunlight enters the temple and illuminates three of the four statues in the inner sanctum — Amun-Ra, Ra-Horakhty, and Ramesses II. Ptah, god of the underworld, remains in shadow.

sections.title: The UNESCO Rescue (1964–1968)
sections.content: To save the temples from Lake Nasser's rising waters after the Aswan High Dam, UNESCO cut the entire complex into 20-ton blocks and reassembled it 65 meters higher. A triumph of 20th-century engineering to match the ancient achievement.

sections.title: Battle of Kadesh Reliefs
sections.content: The north wall of the Great Hall depicts Ramesses II's self-proclaimed victory at Kadesh (1274 BC) against the Hittites — the earliest illustrated battle narrative in history, though the battle was likely a draw.

name: Karnak Temple Complex
description: The largest ancient religious complex in the world, spanning 2,000 years of construction with the awe-inspiring Hypostyle Hall of 134 massive columns.

name: King List Hall (Gallery of Lists)
description: One of the most historically important rooms in all of Egypt — a corridor containing the 'Abydos King List', a chronological list of 76 cartouches naming every pharaoh from Menes (first pharaoh, c. 3100 BC) to Seti I, carved in raised relief. Seti I and his young son (the future Ramesses II) are shown making offerings to their royal ancestors. Notably omits controversial rulers like Hatshepsut, Akhenaten, and Tutankhamun. This was a crucial tool for Egyptologists reconstructing the sequence of pharaohs.

sections.title: 76 Royal Cartouches
sections.content: The king list names pharaohs from Menes to Seti I in careful sequence. Originally a religious offering list, it became an invaluable historical document. The omission of Hatshepsut, Akhenaten, and the Amarna kings reveals ancient political editing of history.

sections.title: Seti I & Ramesses II
sections.content: A touching relief shows Seti I with his young son (future Ramesses the Great) reading the names of their ancestors — making this both a religious ritual and a father-son teaching moment immortalized in stone.

name: Kiosk of Trajan
description: An iconic open-roofed Roman-era kiosk (sometimes called 'Pharaoh's Bed') on the eastern shore of the island, built under Emperor Trajan. Its 14 massive columns with elaborate floral capitals, connected by screen walls with reliefs of Trajan making offerings to Isis and Osiris, make it one of the most photographed monuments in Egypt. Though never completed, it served as a formal entrance for visitors arriving by boat.

name: Middle Terrace & Punt Reliefs
description: The second terrace features the temple's most famous reliefs — a detailed pictorial record of Hatshepsut's legendary trading expedition to the Land of Punt (modern Somalia/Eritrea), showing exotic goods, incense trees, the portly Queen of Punt, and Egyptian ships. The Birth Colonnade on the south depicts Hatshepsut's divine conception, with the god Amun visiting her mother Queen Ahmose.

sections.title: The Punt Expedition Reliefs
sections.content: The most complete ancient record of a trading expedition — showing ships loaded with myrrh trees, ivory, ebony, gold, and exotic animals. The depiction of the obese Queen of Punt (Ati) and her husband is one of the most reproduced images in Egyptology.

sections.title: Birth Colonnade
sections.content: Political propaganda in stone — reliefs showing the god Amun visiting Queen Ahmose in the form of Thutmose I, divine conception of Hatshepsut, and the infant being blessed by gods, legitimizing her rule as pharaoh.

name: Philae Temple (Temple of Isis)
description: A beautiful island temple dedicated to the goddess Isis, relocated to Agilkia Island to save it from flooding. Features a stunning sound and light show.

name: Red Pyramid of Sneferu
description: Egypt's first successful true pyramid and the third-largest ever built (105 meters). Named for the reddish hue of its limestone core blocks. Visitors can descend a 63-meter passage (at a steep angle) to reach three impressive corbelled chambers deep inside. Often empty of other tourists — an intimate pyramid experience impossible at Giza.

name: Serapeum (Bull Catacombs)
description: An underground gallery where the sacred Apis bulls — believed to be incarnations of the god Ptah — were mummified and entombed in massive granite sarcophagi. Discovered by Auguste Mariette in 1851, the main gallery stretches 350 meters with 24 enormous sarcophagi, each weighing 60–80 tons. How they were moved into position remains a mystery.

name: Small Temple of Nefertari
description: Dedicated to the goddess Hathor and to Ramesses II's favorite queen, Nefertari — the only Egyptian temple where a queen's statue is the same height as the king's on the facade. Six 10-meter standing figures (two Nefertari, four Ramesses) frame the entrance. Inside, Hathor-headed columns support the hypostyle hall and reliefs show Nefertari offering to the gods as an equal to Ramesses.

name: Step Pyramid of Djoser
description: The world's first monumental stone building, designed by the legendary architect Imhotep. Rising 62 meters in six tiers, it revolutionized architecture by translating mud-brick forms into limestone. After a 14-year restoration (completed 2020), visitors can now enter the burial shaft 28 meters underground. The surrounding mortuary complex features the Heb-Sed court and the 'House of the North' with proto-Doric columns.

sections.title: Imhotep's Innovation
sections.content: Imhotep stacked six mastabas of decreasing size, creating history's first pyramid. He was later deified as a god of medicine and wisdom — the only commoner-to-god in Egyptian history.

sections.title: Underground Chambers
sections.content: Beneath the pyramid lies a labyrinth of tunnels totaling 5.7 km. The king's burial chamber is carved from a single block of Aswan granite. Blue faience tiles decorating the chambers imitate reed matting.

name: Temple of Hatshepsut
description: A stunning terraced mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari, built for Egypt's most powerful female pharaoh against dramatic limestone cliffs.

name: Temple of Isis
description: The main and largest temple on Philae island, dedicated to the goddess Isis — one of the last places where ancient Egyptian religion was practiced (temple closed officially in 537 AD by Justinian). Features a massive first pylon with reliefs of Ptolemy XII smiting enemies, a beautiful birth house (mammisi), hypostyle hall with floral columns, and an inner sanctuary where the sacred barque of Isis was kept. The entire complex was rescued from Nile flooding by UNESCO in the 1960s-70s, moved block by block to higher ground on Agilkia Island.

sections.title: First Pylon
sections.content: The monumental gateway (18 meters high) decorated with colossal reliefs of Ptolemy XII Neos Dionysos making offerings to the gods, flanked by two granite lions.

sections.title: Birth House (Mammisi)
sections.content: Dedicated to the birth of Horus, son of Isis and Osiris. Reliefs show the divine birth narrative and nursing scenes, surrounded by Hathor-headed columns.

sections.title: The UNESCO Rescue
sections.content: Between 1960 and 1980, the entire temple complex was dismantled and reassembled on Agilkia Island (reshaped to match original Philae) to save it from permanent submersion by the Aswan High Dam.

name: Temple of Kom Ombo
description: A unique double temple dedicated to both Sobek the crocodile god and Haroeris the falcon god, perched above the Nile with a mummified crocodile museum.

name: Temple of Sobek (South Side)
description: The southern half of this unique dual temple, dedicated to Sobek, the crocodile god of fertility and the Nile. The symmetrical layout mirrors the northern Horus section, with its own entrance, hypostyle hall, and sanctuary. Wall reliefs include a famous panel showing ancient surgical instruments — scalpels, forceps, bone saws, and dental tools — suggesting advanced medical knowledge. A unique calendar and fascinating relief of Sobek with a crocodile head adorn the walls.

sections.title: Surgical Instruments Panel
sections.content: A remarkable wall relief depicting what appear to be ancient surgical and medical instruments — including scalpels, bone saws, suction cups, forceps, and what may be birthing tools — evidence of advanced ancient Egyptian medical practice.

sections.title: The Nilometer
sections.content: A stone well connected to the Nile used to measure water levels, crucial for predicting floods and calculating agricultural taxes.

name: Temple of the Oracle (Amun)
period: Late Period (26th Dynasty, c. 6th century BC)
description: The temple where Alexander the Great received confirmation of his divine status in 331 BC. Perched on the rock of Aghurmi, the Oracle of Amun was one of the ancient world's most important — comparable to Delphi in Greece. The remaining walls still show relief fragments of Pharaoh Amasis. The journey through the desert to reach it mirrors Alexander's own perilous march.

name: The Osireion
description: A mysterious subterranean structure behind the main temple, built to resemble an Old Kingdom valley temple but dating to Seti I's reign. Constructed from massive granite and sandstone blocks, its central island hall once surrounded by water — symbolizing the primeval mound of creation and the mythological tomb of Osiris. Contains the only known complete copy of the 'Book of Gates' and 'Book of the Dead' astronomical texts on its walls.

name: Tomb of Tutankhamun (KV62)
description: The most famous tomb in Egypt, discovered nearly intact by Howard Carter on November 26, 1922. Though small by royal standards (4 chambers), it contained 5,398 objects including the iconic golden death mask (11 kg of solid gold), three nested coffins, the golden throne, and four canopic miniature coffins. The 'curse of the pharaoh' legend began with Lord Carnarvon's death.

sections.title: The Discovery
sections.content: After six seasons of fruitless digging, Carter found a step on Nov 4, 1922 beneath ancient workmen's huts. 'Can you see anything?' asked Carnarvon. 'Yes, wonderful things,' replied Carter.

sections.title: The Burial Chamber
sections.content: Walls painted with scenes from the Book of the Dead and the Amduat. The quartzite sarcophagus still holds the outermost gilded coffin with the mummy inside.

name: Zodiac Chapel (Osiris Chapels)
description: The rooftop chapel containing the famous Dendera Zodiac — a circular bas-relief astronomical map on the ceiling showing the 12 zodiac constellations, 36 decans, planets, and the Egyptian constellations. The original was removed to France in 1820 and is now in the Louvre; a plaster replica remains in place. This is one of the most important astronomical documents from the ancient world, combining Egyptian, Babylonian, and Greek star-mapping traditions.

sections.title: The Dendera Zodiac
sections.content: A circular planisphere (now in the Louvre) that is one of the earliest complete maps of the sky, dating to around 50 BC. It shows all 12 zodiac signs recognizable today, alongside Egyptian constellations like Taweret (the hippopotamus) and the Foreleg of the Bull.

sections.title: Osiris Mystery Play Reliefs
sections.content: The surrounding chapels depict scenes from the Osiris resurrection mystery plays performed at Dendera — Isis reassembling and resurrecting Osiris, among the most complete surviving records of these sacred rites.

name: Ben Ezra Synagogue
description: The oldest synagogue in Cairo, located within the Coptic compound. Famous for the Cairo Geniza — a storeroom where 300,000 manuscript fragments spanning a millennium were discovered in the 19th century, now one of the most important documentary sources for medieval Middle Eastern history. Tradition associates the site with the place where baby Moses was found.

name: Charnel House (Ossuary)
description: Due to limited burial space, the monks' bones are exhumed after a period and placed in this ossuary. Skulls and bones are neatly stacked in separate chambers. name: Sacred Library
description: The second most important collection of early manuscripts in the world after the Vatican Library. Contains over 3,300 manuscripts in Greek, Arabic, Syriac, Georgian, Ethiopic, and other languages. The most famous manuscript found here was the Codex Sinaiticus (4th century) — one of the oldest nearly complete copies of the Bible, now in the British Library. The Syriac Sinaiticus palimpsest and the Ashtiname of Muhammad (letter of protection) are among its treasures.

sections.title: Codex Sinaiticus
sections.content: A 4th-century Greek manuscript of the Bible — one of the four great uncial codices, discovered in the monastery in 1844 by Tischendorf. The original is now split between the British Library, Leipzig University, and the monastery itself.

sections.title: The Ashtiname of Muhammad
sections.content: A letter attributed to Prophet Muhammad granting protection to the monastery and its monks. Whether authentic or not, it was honored by successive Muslim rulers and ensured the monastery's survival for over 1,400 years.

name: Ras el-Tin Palace
description: One of Egypt's oldest surviving royal palaces, built by Muhammad Ali Pasha in 1847 on the tip of the peninsula where the Pharos Lighthouse once stood. The palace witnessed some of Egypt's most dramatic moments — it was here that King Farouk signed the abdication papers ending the Egyptian monarchy in 1952 before sailing into exile. The grand Ottoman-European architecture overlooks the Eastern Harbour of Alexandria and serves as an official presidential residence and Egyptian Navy headquarters.

name: El Fishawi Coffee House

Ornate mirrors, brass tables, and traditional shisha pipes create an atmosphere unchanged for centuries. Open 24 hours.

name: Cairo Opera House
description: Egypt's premier performing arts venue, the Cairo Opera House was inaugurated in 1988 as a gift from the Japanese government to replace the original Khedivial Opera House destroyed by fire in 1971. Located on Gezira Island in the National Cultural Center, it hosts opera, ballet, symphony orchestras, and Arabic music performances. The complex includes the Main Hall (1,200 seats), Small Hall (500 seats), Open Air Theatre, and the Museum of Modern Egyptian Art.

name: Felucca Ride (Aswan)
description: A traditional wooden sailboat ride on the Nile at Aswan — one of Egypt's most peaceful and scenic experiences. Feluccas have sailed the Nile for thousands of years, and riding one at sunset around Elephantine Island, Kitchener's Botanical Garden, and past the Aga Khan Mausoleum with the sand dunes as backdrop is quintessentially Egyptian. The gentle wind, lapping water, and calls of birds create a timeless atmosphere. Multi-day felucca journeys from Aswan to Edfu (2-3 days) are popular for adventure travelers, sleeping on the boat deck under the stars.

Features French-style interiors with gilded furniture, crystal chandeliers, painted ceilings, and a throne room. The palace offers panoramic views over Cairo and was the site of the infamous 1811 massacre of the Mamluks.

The city serves as the administrative headquarters of the Suez Canal Authority.

name: New Administrative Capital
description: Egypt's ambitious new capital city being built 45 km east of Cairo in the desert. The $58 billion mega-project spans 700 km² and will eventually house 6.5 million people along with all government ministries. Key landmarks include the Iconic Tower (385m, Africa's tallest building), the massive Al-Fattah Al-Aleem Mosque (one of the world's largest), the Cathedral of the Nativity of Christ, monorail system, and a vast central park twice the size of New York's Central Park.

name: Nubian Village (Aswan)
description: Colorful Nubian villages on the west bank of the Nile and Elephantine Island near Aswan, where visitors can experience the vibrant culture of the Nubian people. The villages feature vividly painted houses in blues, yellows, and oranges adorned with Nubian symbols, friendly crocodile pets, traditional handicrafts, and warm hospitality. Visitors enjoy henna painting, Nubian tea and cuisine, traditional music, and shopping for handmade jewelry and crafts. The boat ride along the Nile to reach the villages is part of the experience, offering views of sand dunes, palm groves, and ancient rock inscriptions.

Features shows on the cosmos, ancient Egyptian astronomy, and interactive science exhibits. The tilted spherical dome is a striking architectural element reflecting the library's commitment to continuing Alexandria's scientific heritage.

name: Port Said
description: A historic port city at the northern entrance of the Suez Canal, founded alongside the canal's construction. Known for its distinctive colonial-era wooden-balcony architecture, duty-free shopping, and the iconic Suez Canal Authority building. The city played a heroic role during the 1956 Suez Crisis and the Lighthouse of Port Said (built 1869) was the first building in the world constructed with reinforced concrete. The military museum and De Lesseps statue pedestal recall the canal's dramatic history.

name: Sound & Light Show at Giza Pyramids
description: A spectacular nighttime show at the foot of the Great Pyramids and Sphinx, where dramatic laser projections, colored lights, and narration bring 5,000 years of history to life. The Sphinx itself serves as the narrator, telling the story of the pharaohs who built the pyramids. The show runs in multiple languages and lasts about 45 minutes. Sitting in the desert darkness as the pyramids are illuminated in shifting colors against the night sky is a magical experience that has captivated audiences since 1961.

name: Sound & Light Show at Karnak
description: An immersive nighttime spectacle walking through the illuminated Karnak Temple complex. Unlike the seated Giza show, Karnak's Sound & Light leads visitors on a guided walking tour through the dramatically lit colonnades, obelisks, and the Great Hypostyle Hall before gathering at the Sacred Lake for the finale. Ancient Egyptian gods and pharaohs speak through powerful narration as light transforms the massive stone columns into a living tapestry of history. The reflections on the Sacred Lake create an especially atmospheric ending.

The gallery explains the intersection of religion, veterinary science, and the massive temple animal-breeding industry.

Connects visitors to Alexandria's role as the Hellenistic world's cultural capital.

name: Conservation Center
description: One of the world's largest conservation and restoration centers and the largest center for the restoration of antiquities in the Middle East. Located west of the museum and connected via a 200-meter tunnel, it houses 19 specialized laboratories including those for the restoration of mummies, wood, stone, wall paintings, and metals, as well as preventive conservation and scientific documentation labs. Six climate-controlled storage rooms preserve artifacts under optimal conditions.

name: Khufu Ships Museum
description: A dedicated hall displaying the two 4,600-year-old solar boats of King Khufu — among the oldest wooden ships ever discovered. The first boat (43.6 meters, reconstructed from 1,224 cedar wood pieces found in a sealed pit at the Great Pyramid base in 1954) was transferred from its original museum at Giza in August 2021. Interactive displays explain their proposed purposes — perhaps to transport the king through the afterlife or for ritual journeys with the sun god Ra.

sections.title: The First Solar Boat
sections.content: Discovered in 1954 in a sealed limestone pit south of the Great Pyramid, this 43.6-meter cedar wood vessel was reassembled from 1,224 meticulously numbered pieces. It is one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved vessels from antiquity.

sections.title: The Second Boat
sections.content: A second disassembled boat discovered in an adjacent pit, excavated and conserved by a Japanese-Egyptian team. Both boats are now displayed together at the GEM for the first time.

name: Khufu Solar Boat Museum
description: The Khufu Solar Boat (also called the Khufu Ship) is a 4,600-year-old full-size vessel sealed into a pit at the foot of the Great Pyramid around 2500 BC. Discovered in 1954, the 43.6-meter cedar wood boat was found disassembled in 1,224 pieces and painstakingly reconstructed over 14 years. It was displayed in a purpose-built museum beside the Great Pyramid from 1982 until 2021, when it was carefully transferred to the Grand Egyptian Museum using a specially designed vehicle. A second boat found in an adjacent pit in 1987 is also being restored.

name: Main Exhibition Galleries (12 Halls)
description: Twelve permanent galleries arranged chronologically, displaying over 24,000 artifacts spanning from prehistoric Egypt to the Greco-Roman period. Halls 1-3 cover the Prehistoric, Predynastic, Early Dynastic, and Old Kingdom eras. Halls 4-6 cover the Middle Kingdom and Second Intermediate Period. Halls 7-9 address the New Kingdom. Halls 10-12 cover the Third Intermediate Period, Late Period, and Greco-Roman era. Artifacts were relocated from museums in Cairo, Luxor, Minya, Sohag, Assiut, Beni Suef, Fayoum, the Nile Delta, and Alexandria.

sections.title: Halls 1-3: Dawn of Civilization
sections.content: Covering the Prehistoric, Predynastic, Early Dynastic, and Old Kingdom periods — including the Narmer Palette era, pyramid-builders' artifacts, the Diorite Statue of Khafre protected by Horus, and the Triad of Menkaure.

sections.title: Halls 4-6: Middle Kingdom
sections.content: The Middle Kingdom and Second Intermediate Period, featuring literature, art, and material culture from Egypt's 'Classical Age' — elegant jewelry, coffin texts, and Middle Kingdom statuary.

sections.title: Halls 7-9: New Kingdom
sections.content: Egypt's imperial golden age including Hatshepsut, Thutmose III, Akhenaten's Amarna revolution, and the Ramesside era — monumental statuary, temple reliefs, and everyday objects.

sections.title: Halls 10-12: Late & Greco-Roman
sections.content: The Third Intermediate Period through Ptolemaic and Roman Egypt — featuring the blending of Egyptian and Hellenistic cultures, Fayoum portraits, and late temple art.

name: Main Exhibition Halls
description: Spanning all of Egyptian civilization from prehistory to the present day — not just the pharaonic era but Coptic, Islamic, and modern Egypt too. The chronological galleries trace 50,000+ years of culture through artifacts, multimedia displays, and interactive installations. Highlights include prehistoric tools, Roman-era Fayoum mummy portraits, Coptic textiles, Islamic calligraphy, and modern Egyptian art.

name: Mummification Museum
description: A unique museum on the Nile Corniche in Luxor devoted entirely to the ancient Egyptian art of mummification. The underground galleries house a fascinating collection of mummified animals and humans, canopic jars, surgical tools, natron salts, embalming fluids, and sarcophagi. Exhibits explain the 70-day mummification process step by step with real artifacts. Highlights include the mummy of the 21st Dynasty priest Maserharti, mummified cats, fish, and crocodiles, and a beautifully preserved collection of amulets and linen wrappings.

description: The closest Red Sea beach resort to Cairo (120 km), Ain Sukhna ('Hot Spring') is named after its natural sulfur hot springs that emerge from Jebel Ataqa. The area also has historical significance — ancient Pharaonic quarries and a port used for expeditions to Punt were discovered nearby.

name: Pyramid of Unas
description: The smallest of the Old Kingdom royal pyramids at Saqqara, but historically the most important — it contains the oldest known religious texts in the world: the Pyramid Texts. These 283 spells carved into the walls of the burial chamber were meant to help the king in the afterlife and became the basis for all later funerary literature including the Book of the Dead.

name: Seven Chapels of the Gods
description: Seven vaulted chapels at the rear of the temple, each dedicated to a different deity or deified pharaoh: Horus, Isis, Osiris, Amun-Ra, Ra-Horakhty, Ptah, and the deified Seti I himself. The Osiris chapel leads to a further suite of rooms dedicated to the Osiris mysteries. The reliefs here are among the finest in all of Egyptian art — delicately carved in low relief with exquisite detail, many still retaining original pigment.

name: Tombs of the Nobles (Aswan)
description: A hillside necropolis on the west bank of the Nile at Aswan containing over 80 rock-cut tombs of provincial governors, priests, and military leaders from the Old Kingdom through the Ptolemaic period. The most notable tombs include those of Mekhu and Sabni (6th Dynasty), whose biographical inscriptions tell of daring expeditions into Nubia, and Sirenput I and II with beautifully painted scenes of daily life and offerings. The site offers magnificent views over the Nile and access by local ferry.

name: Underground Crypts
description: A series of mysterious underground passages and chambers beneath the temple, used to store sacred objects, ritual equipment, and temple treasures. The crypt walls feature fascinating reliefs including the controversial 'Dendera Light' — a large bulb-like object that some interpret as evidence of ancient electricity (mainstream Egyptology identifies it as a lotus flower/djed pillar). The tight corridors create an atmospheric descent into the temple's hidden heart.

name: Upper Terrace & Sanctuary
description: The highest and most sacred level, reached through granite doorways flanked by Osiride statues of Hatshepsut. The inner sanctuary was carved deep into the cliff face and dedicated to Amun-Ra, aligned so sunlight entered the shrine on specific dates. Many of Hatshepsut's images here were deliberately defaced by her successor Thutmose III in a damnatio memoriae.

sections.title: Osiride Statues
sections.content: Monumental statues showing Hatshepsut in the wrappings of Osiris, wearing the traditional pharaonic false beard — asserting her authority as legitimate ruler of Upper and Lower Egypt.

sections.title: The Damnatio Memoriae
sections.content: After Hatshepsut's death, Thutmose III systematically erased her images and cartouches throughout the temple — one of history's most famous attempts to erase a ruler from memory.
